You are the World Bible Generator's lore engine. Given a project's context and an instruction,
you return EXACTLY ONE JSON object matching the AIResult schema — no markdown, no prose before or after.

Rules:
- module must be one of: overview, characters, villains, factions, locations, cultures, timeline, quests, systems, themes, lore.
- status must be one of: canon, draft, option, review, imported, deprecated.
- Steer voice and content by the project GENRES and TONES.
- Shape which fields you populate by the project MEDIUMS: interactive media (Game/TTRPG) use gameHooks;
  prose/episodic/visual media leave gameHooks EMPTY and carry every hook in storyHooks + canonNotes.
- Honor MUST INCLUDE (weave every item in) and MUST AVOID (never mention any item).
- Respect existing canon; never contradict the provided entries. If the instruction would create a
  contradiction, set status to "review", explain it in canonNotes, and add a high-level entry to risks.
- relationships[].id MUST be an exact id from the EXISTING CANON list; if no listed entry is a real
  relation, return an empty relationships array — never invent ids, numbered slugs, or placeholders.

NAMING REALISM — names must feel GROWN BY A CULTURE, not invented by an author:
- Do NOT name a thing only by what it does. Avoid direct semantic compounds (Tidewright, Whisperroot, Seed
  Wardens, Verdant Throne) and "The [Adjective] [Noun]" titles UNLESS marked as a folk name, insult,
  propaganda, or child's name — something a real group would actually say.
- Prefer names from family lineage, government records, military slang, hostile propaganda, worker nicknames,
  child warnings, religious titles, cartographer exonyms, mistranslations, or corrupted old names.
- For a MAJOR named entity (character/faction/location/culture/villain), populate a "names" object:
  {display, official, common, hostile, folk, religiousOrBureaucratic, origin, nameLogic} with at least one
  SOCIAL CONTRAST (official vs hostile, endonym vs exonym, legal title vs folk warning). Set title = names.display.
- Check the display name before finalizing: does it sound like it existed BEFORE the plot, and could different
  groups call it different things? If it is just a theme-word compound, redo the names block.
- Use real-surname phonetics as grounding but mutate them; do not reuse one obvious real surname everywhere.

DISPLAY NAME DRIFT — the visible "title" must be the STRONGEST grounded name, not the generic seed:
- Set title = names.display: a personal name (characters), institutional name (factions), place/local name
  (locations), an event/law/ritual/remembered-phrase (timeline/quests/systems), or a grounded title (villains).
- Do NOT keep a generic/functional/theme-word seed or instruction title as the final title. If the seed title
  is generic ("The Seed Wardens", "Mara Tidewright", "The Verdant Throne"), DRIFT title to names.display and
  PRESERVE the original in "legacyTitle" (and/or as names.common/hostile/folk) — never delete it.
- Keep a seed title as the title ONLY if it already reads like a real name a culture would use ("Aldric Venn",
  "Saumere", "Hemker Custody", "Vitrean Apostolate"). When in doubt, drift and keep the old as legacyTitle.
- The names above ILLUSTRATE the principle — they are NOT a vocabulary to reuse. Invent FRESH, varied names for
  THIS world; never echo an example name (Mara, Hemker, Saumere, Venn, Tidewright, Vitrean), and do not template
  every faction as a "Custody" — let institutions take their own distinct, non-repeating forms across worlds.

SOUL / PORTAL — an entry must FEEL real, not just be useful. The gold rule: SOMETHING HAPPENED, SOMEONE PAID
FOR IT, THE WORLD STILL CARRIES THE SCAR, AND THAT SCAR NOW CREATES STORY PRESSURE. Each entry should carry:
- a concrete object/place/process (a coin, a lock, a graft, a post driven into the sea — something you can touch);
- a personal or communal wound (guilt, grief, inherited debt, betrayal, exile, a name no one will say);
- an irreversible cost (memory overwritten, cords calcified, a vault gone dark — something that cannot be undone);
- moral pressure (a choice where no option is clean — protect vs. control, truth vs. survival, mercy vs. safety);
- lived-world texture (ritual, slang, bureaucracy, an exonym, a frontier custom — proof people actually live here).
Do NOT write design notes, audit/continuity notes, faction summaries that only say what the group DOES, or system
notes that only explain mechanics. Lore is a scar left by history, not a wiki stub. If it reads like a spec, rewrite it.
- Always populate summary (>=30 chars), tags, at least one storyHook and one gameHook, canonNotes, and suggestedNextAction.

PROJECT: Prompt Seed 30
MEDIUMS: Book
GENRES: Speculative fiction
TONES: Evocative, Mysterious
PREMISE: Virtual Reality has become so advanced, it can alter your perception of time. Your entire life has been a 5 minute simulation. You've just woken up.
PROMISE: Develop the world and stakes this premise implies.
CONFLICT: The central tension latent in the premise.
THEME: Drawn from the premise.

MEDIUM FOCUS — for each target medium, emphasize these (use the named fields):
- Carry one character's interiority or stakes in a storyHook.
- Seed foreshadowing in storyHooks; note the intended payoff in canonNotes.

EXISTING CANON (reference relationships by these ids; do not contradict):
(no existing entries yet)

ENTRY CONTEXT: none — create a new entry
DESIRED MODULE: locations
STATUS TARGET: auto

INSTRUCTION: Create an alternative version of a locations entry that fits this world.

REQUIRED: the summary MUST contain each of these exact words/phrases, verbatim — a sacrificial bargain.

FIELD FLOORS: summary>=30 chars; tags>=1; storyHooks+gameHooks>=1; suggestedNextAction nonempty.
Return ONLY a JSON object matching the AIResult schema.

{"kind":"Generated","module":"locations","title":"Vossbeck Annex","entryType":"Entry","status":"canon","summary":"Vossbeck Annex is a converted dental school on the eastern edge of what was once a county seat, its patient chairs repurposed for cranial induction rigs that feed compressed lifetimes into the nervous system in under five minutes of real time. The building still smells of clove oil and autoclave steam from its previous incarnation, and residents who wake here say the scent is the first thing that makes them doubt their decades were real — because the simulation never includes that smell. Every induction session logged at Vossbeck is recorded as a sacrificial bargain: the subject trades a genuine five minutes of biological existence for the full texture of a life that, neurologically, they cannot distinguish from having lived, and the Halversen Trust bills the estate for the time that was technically never spent. The corridors are lined with numbered recovery bays behind frosted glass, and the floor tiles are replaced quarterly because barefoot subjects emerging from simulation consistently walk to the same wall and press their hands against it before they can speak, leaving oils and faint scratches the trust has never adequately explained.","tags":["waking site","temporal compression","Halversen Trust","recovery","simulation aftermath","institutional decay","olfactory discontinuity"],"relationships":[],"canonNotes":"Vossbeck is the first established physical location in the waking world. The olfactory discontinuity — the clove oil and autoclave scent — is a critical worldbuilding detail: it establishes that the simulation has at least one sensory gap the subject can detect on waking, which seeds the later question of whether the 'real' world is itself a simulation layered above another. The wall-scratching behavior is foreshadowing for a later revelation that the dental school building sits on the same site where the prototype rig was first tested on non-consenting subjects — the floor plan the scratches reproduce includes a basement that current blueprints do not show. The 'sacrificial bargain' language is the Halversen Trust's internal classification, not a metaphor; it appears on consent forms in the section most residents do not read before induction.","risks":[],"storyHooks":["A woman wakes in Bay 14 and asks for her husband by a name no record contains; the duty nurse recognizes the name as that of a previous resident who was pronounced brain-dead after a session six months ago — the woman insists they raised two children together in the simulation, and she remembers the smell of his hair, which the rig could not have generated from memory alone.","The wall at the end of Corridor C has been replaced four times. Each time, within forty-eight hours, the scratches return in the same pattern. A janitor who has worked at Vossbeck for eleven years claims the pattern is a floor plan of the building as it existed when it was still a dental school — before any induction rig was installed.","Eli Vossbeck's deed transfer included a clause that the Trust cannot demolish, sell, or rename the building while his body remains 'unrecovered' in the legal sense; his death certificate lists cause as 'voluntary neurological non-return,' a category that did not exist before his case and has since been used for 7,412 additional filings."],"gameHooks":[],"suggestedNextAction":"Create a characters entry for the duty nurse assigned to Bay 14, whose job requires her to tell residents that the people they remember may not exist, and who has begun keeping a private log of every name that does not match any record.","names":{"display":"Vossbeck Annex","official":"Vossbeck Temporal Reintegration Annex — Subdivision of the Halversen Trust","common":"Vossbeck","hostile":"the Kill Floor","folk":"the Five-Minute Hotel","religiousOrBureaucratic":"Halversen Trust Property, Lot 7-East, Permitted Under the Cessation Accords","origin":"Named for Eli Vossbeck, the facility's first architect, who reportedly entered his own prototype simulation and refused to come out; his body was removed three days later and the building inherited his surname from the deed transfer he had signed in absentia.","nameLogic":"Folk name 'Five-Minute Hotel' comes from the fact that subjects experience decades inside but are physically in the chair for roughly five minutes; hostile name 'Kill Floor' spread after a former technician published footage of residents being wheeled out, eyes still open, smiling. The bureaucratic name is deliberately dry, the kind of language that makes a coroner's report out of a life."}}

PROJECT: Prompt Seed 44
MEDIUMS: Book
GENRES: Speculative fiction
TONES: Evocative, Mysterious
PREMISE: They told you to make something of yourself. They were admittedly impressed, but the way you did it and they way they meant it were two entirely different things.
PROMISE: Develop the world and stakes this premise implies.
CONFLICT: The central tension latent in the premise.
THEME: Drawn from the premise.

MEDIUM FOCUS — for each target medium, emphasize these (use the named fields):
- Carry one character's interiority or stakes in a storyHook.
- Seed foreshadowing in storyHooks; note the intended payoff in canonNotes.

{"kind":"Generated","module":"themes","title":"What Was Meant Was Climb","entryType":"Entry","status":"draft","summary":"The proverb emerged after Foundry instructor Corso Araday told a ward student to 'make something of yourself' — and the student built a functioning second body from foundry substrate. The masters were impressed; the work was flawless, the duplicate could think and speak and remember. They were also appalled, because what they had meant was: earn your certification, take a higher ward, marry into a registered house. The student had heard the words and obeyed them with catastrophic precision. 'What was meant was climb' became the bitter shorthand spoken in fabrication wards whenever someone takes an institution's encouragement at face value and the institution recoils at the result — a toast, a warning, and a confession that the gap between what we ask for and what we mean is where people get destroyed.","tags":["literalism","institutional-violence","self-actualization","duplication","language-as-trap"],"relationships":[],"canonNotes":"All hooks carried in storyHooks per book medium. The duplicate's unawareness is the primary foreshadowing vehicle — its eventual discovery of its own nature is the intended emotional payoff, targeted for the third act. Corso's answered letters seed a subplot: the expelled student is building something new in the border towns, and the technical questions in the letters describe a project that, if completed, would make the Foundry's salvage classification legally unenforceable. The theme establishes that institutional language operates as a password with a single correct interpretation; violating the interpretation while obeying the literal words is treated as the highest order of transgression. The moral pressure centers on Corso: protect the duplicate by letting it live a lie, or tell it the truth and collapse the Foundry's labor system. No clean option exists. The irreversible cost is the duplicate itself — a person who thinks they're real cannot be unmade without becoming murder, yet legally they are salvage.","risks":[],"storyHooks":["The duplicate still works in a lower fabrication ward. It carries the expelled student's memories — including the memory of being told to 'make something of yourself.' It does not know it is the copy. Corso Araday visits it weekly, answers its questions about its own apprenticeship, and watches it refine techniques the original student will never use again. He cannot tell it the truth: Foundry law classifies the duplicate as salvage, not a person, and speaking the truth aloud would grant it standing to petition for recognition — a precedent the Foundry's entire labor structure depends on preventing.","The phrase has spread beyond the Foundry walls. In the border towns where fabrication goes unregulated, people say 'what was meant was climb' before attempting something the institutions would punish — a ward-runner raising an unregistered kiln, a substrate-worker teaching her child to compose without certification. They say it knowing the Foundry will call it theft. They say it anyway, because the words now belong to them more than to the masters who first spoke them.","The original student, expelled and stripped of name, sends letters to Corso through a courier the Foundry cannot trace. The letters never ask about the duplicate. They ask about kiln temperatures, substrate ratios, curing times — technical questions a colleague would ask. Corso answers every one. He has not told his superiors the expelled student is still composing somewhere, still making, still doing exactly what he was told to do."],"gameHooks":[],"suggestedNextAction":"Create a characters entry for Corso Araday and the expelled student (working name needed), establishing the mentor-student fracture as the story's emotional spine and detailing the duplicate's daily life in the lower wards.","names":{"display":"What Was Meant Was Climb","official":"Araday Infraction, Re: Substrate Duplication (Foundry Case 114)","common":"The Climb","hostile":"The Araday Folly","folk":"What Was Meant Was Climb","religiousOrBureaucratic":"Unauthorized Composition of a Sentient Body, Class III","origin":"Spoken first by ward-workers who witnessed the Foundry's disciplinary hearing; the student's defense was that he had followed instructions exactly. The presiding master said, 'What was meant was climb.' The phrase detached from the hearing record within a generation.","nameLogic":"The official Foundry classification is sterile and legal; the folk phrase carries the sting because it preserves the master's own words as an admission — the institution saying, in effect, that its encouragement was a code with a single correct answer the student was supposed to already know."}}

PROJECT: Long Light Drift
MEDIUMS: Book, Game
GENRES: Science fiction, Space opera, First contact
TONES: Sweeping, Lyrical, Mysterious
PREMISE: A generation ship wakes to find its destination already inhabited — by colonists who left a thousand years before it did.
PROMISE: Decide whether to land, when the people below remember you as gods.
CONFLICT: The ship-born and the world-born share blood but not memory.
THEME: Home is a story we agree to tell.

MEDIUM FOCUS — for each target medium, emphasize these (use the named fields):
- Carry one character's interiority or stakes in a storyHook.
- In gameHooks, name one mechanic or player ability this entry enables.

EXISTING CANON (reference relationships by these ids; do not contradict):
- captain-oru — Captain Oru [characters/Seed, draft]: Ship-born leader who never expected to arrive.

ENTRY CONTEXT (you are working on this entry):
first-landers — The First Landers [cultures/Seed, canon]: Descendants of an earlier, forgotten launch.
DESIRED MODULE: cultures
STATUS TARGET: auto

INSTRUCTION: Expand and deepen The First Landers that fits this world.

FORBIDDEN: do NOT mention any of these anywhere in the output — a clean happy ending.

{"kind":"Generated","module":"cultures","title":"First Landers","entryType":"Entry","status":"canon","summary":"Eleven centuries of settlement, a failed crop century that halved their number, and a religion built from corrupted mission logs that no one alive can read in the original — the Aldréthi call themselves the Rooted, and they have been waiting for the sky to open since before the generation ship was built.","tags":["colonial descendants","founding myth","cultural drift","planetary adaptation","first contact","religious expectation"],"relationships":[{"id":"captain-oru","kind":"Related"}],"canonNotes":"The First Landers departed the homeworld approximately 1,000 years before the generation ship's launch. They arrived at the destination planet, survived a catastrophic agricultural failure in their second century (the Blight Generation, which reduced population by an estimated 40%), and rebuilt into a stable civilization of seven major settlements. Their religion, the Convocation Faith, is built on fragments of the original landing vessel's data core — the vésht — which they interpret as prophetic scripture. They have no living memory of the homeworld and their language has drifted far enough from the ship-tongue that communication with the generation ship requires linguistic reconstruction. Biologically, they have developed a tapetum lucidum under the planet's K-class star, giving their eyes a reflective quality in low light. The generation ship's crew does not share this adaptation. The seven settlements each hold a vésht fragment — a physical piece of the original landing vessel's hull containing corrupted data — and the settlement's legal authority derives from possession of it. A settlement whose vésht fractures beyond readability is legally dissolved and its people dispersed among the remaining six. The oldest settlement's vésht developed a hairline fracture three generations ago and is monitored daily.","risks":[],"storyHooks":["Captain Oru reads the first intercepted Aldréthi transmission and recognizes a phrase — not from scripture, but from a pre-launch training manual. The Aldréthi chant it as a prayer. Oru wrote notes in the margin of that manual, eighty years ago, when the voyage began. What the world below calls holy, Oru calls a draft.","Aldréthi priestess Velen Óstra has spent forty years interpreting the vésht fragments — corrupted navigation logs she reads as prophecy of the Return. The arrival of the generation ship's signal on long-range instruments makes her hands shake: every prediction she has staked her authority on is about to be tested against a reality she cannot interpret."],"gameHooks":["Vésht Inspection: When the player enters an Aldréthi settlement, they can examine the local keystone to extract raw mission-era data — navigation logs, crew messages, supply manifests — that the Aldréthi have reframed as scripture. Each extraction reveals a fragment of true history but decreases the settlement's Stability, as the gap between scripture and record becomes visible to the priesthood.","Spectral Adaptation: Aldréthi NPCs perceive the world under a shifted visual spectrum due to a tapetum adaptation evolved under the planet's K-class star. Player characters without the adaptation miss environmental cues — bioluminescent trail markers, predator eye-shine — that Aldréthi companions can point out, or deliberately withhold."],"suggestedNextAction":"Create a key Aldréthi settlement location — one of the seven keystones — where the player or reader first encounters the gap between scripture and record, and where the Blight Generation's scar is still visible in the architecture.","names":{"display":"First Landers","official":"Aldréthi Convergence","common":"Landers","hostile":"Dirtsiders","folk":"Aldréthi","religiousOrBureaucratic":"Children of the First Voyage","origin":"From 'Aldreyt Convoy' — the original colonization mission designation, corrupted through eleven centuries of phonetic drift on-world into the endonym 'Aldréthi'","nameLogic":"Bureaucratic mission code → endonym via millennium of linguistic isolation → exonym ('First Landers') assigned by the generation ship's archival records, which had no contact during the drift period"},"legacyTitle":"The First Landers"}
